questions.question: Explain the difference between valid padding and same padding in CNNs. Give an example of when each would be used.
keyPoints: Valid padding: no padding added, output shrinks; used when spatial reduction is acceptable
Same padding: adds border pixels to maintain input size; used to preserve spatial information at edges
Formula: same padding = (filter_size-1)/2 for odd filters

questions.question: What is transfer learning? Discuss two strategies for applying transfer learning in CNNs.
keyPoints: Transfer learning: using pretrained model weights as starting point
Feature extraction: freeze pretrained layers, train only new classifier
Fine-tuning: unfreeze and train some/all pretrained layers on new data
Choice depends on dataset size and similarity

questions.question: Explain the working of a max pooling layer with a 2x2 window and stride 2. How does it help in CNNs?
keyPoints: Selects maximum value from each 2x2 region
Reduces spatial dimensions by half (downsampling)
Provides translation invariance
Reduces computation and parameters, helps prevent overfitting

questions.question: What are the key innovations introduced by AlexNet that made it successful?
keyPoints: ReLU activation instead of tanh (faster training)
Dropout for regularization
GPU training (faster computation)
Data augmentation
Architecture: 8 layers, 60M parameters, won ImageNet 2012

questions.question: Explain the concept of receptive field in CNNs. Why is it important?
keyPoints: Receptive field: portion of input affecting a neuron's output
Increases with network depth
Larger RF = more global context
Important for understanding feature detection hierarchy
Limits what patterns each neuron can detect

questions.question: Discuss the role of data augmentation in training CNNs. Give three examples of augmentation techniques.
keyPoints: Data augmentation artificially increases training data
Improves model generalization and reduces overfitting
Techniques: horizontal flipping, random cropping, rotation, color jittering, random erasing
Makes model robust to variations in real-world data

questions.question: What is the vanishing gradient problem in deep neural networks? How do modern CNN architectures address it?
keyPoints: Gradients become very small during backpropagation, preventing weight updates
Especially problematic in deep networks
Solutions: ReLU activation, batch normalization, residual connections (ResNet)
Skip connections provide direct gradient path to early layers

questions.question: Explain the architecture of ResNet. How do skip connections help in training very deep networks? Describe the residual learning framework.
keyPoints: ResNet uses residual blocks with skip/shortcut connections
H(x) = F(x) + x, where F(x) is learned residual
Skip connections enable identity mapping when optimal
Solves degradation problem (accuracy decreases with depth)
Allows effective gradient flow through backpropagation
Variants: ResNet-18, 34, 50, 101, 152
Uses bottleneck design in deeper versions for efficiency

questions.question: Compare and contrast three popular CNN architectures: LeNet-5, AlexNet, and VGGNet. Discuss their contributions to the evolution of deep learning for computer vision.
keyPoints: LeNet-5: 1998, handwritten digit recognition, 5 layers, established CNN basics
AlexNet: 2012, ImageNet breakthrough, ReLU, dropout, GPU training, 8 layers
VGGNet: 2014, simplicity with 3x3 convolutions, deeper (16-19 layers)
Evolution: increasing depth, better regularization, more efficient operations
VGG proved small filters stacked are better than large filters

questions.question: Derive the formula for calculating output dimensions in a convolutional layer. Given an input of 224x224x3, 64 filters of size 7x7, stride 2, and valid padding, calculate the output dimensions.
keyPoints: Formula: O = floor((W - F + P)/S) + 1
W = input size, F = filter size, P = padding, S = stride
For 224x224x3, F=7, S=2, P=0 (valid)
Output dimensions: 109x109x64
Important for architecture design and parameter calculation

questions.question: Discuss the importance of hyperparameters in CNNs. Explain how to choose: (i) filter size (3x3 vs 5x5 vs 7x7), (ii) number of filters, (iii) pooling strategy, and (iv) learning rate.
keyPoints: Filter size: smaller (3x3) preferred, stacked for larger RF, fewer parameters
Number of filters: increases with depth, typically powers of 2 (64, 128, 256)
Pooling: max for distinctive features, average for smooth features, sometimes replaced with strided conv
Learning rate: start with 0.01-0.001, use decay, learning rate schedules
Other: batch size, dropout rate, weight decay
